text: [1] Data Privacy Information according to Art. 13, 14 and 21 GDPR: Compliance Whistleblower System (Status:08/2022) In the following, we inform you about the processing of personal data by Forschungszentrum Jülich in the context of the whistleblower system as well as about the associated data protection regulations, claims and rights. Personal data is any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person. An identifiable natural person is one who can be identified, directly or indirectly, in particular by reference to an identifier such as a name, an identification number, location data or an online identifier. Forschungszentrum Jülich uses web-based software, a cloud solution hosted in Germany, which assists in the detection of operational wrongdoing. By implementing such a system, criminal, illegal, morally reprehensible or unfair actions can be detected and prevente

tion of operational wrongdoing. By implementing such a system, criminal, illegal, morally reprehensible or unfair actions can be detected and prevented at an early stage. As a result, incalculable material and immaterial damages as well as reputational damage can be averted. Purpose of Data Processing Forschungszentrum Jülich processes the personal data of the whistleblower(s), unless the whistleblowing was done anonymously, as well as the personal data of the accused person(s), such as name and other communication and content data, exclusively for the purpose of receiving and following up on tips about criminal, illegal, morally reprehensible or unfair acts in a secure and confidential manner. 2. Categories of personal data processed • Information about the whistleblower (unless the whistleblower wishes to remain anonymous) and the accused, such as o first and last name o function/title o contact details o Other personal information related to the employment relationship, if applicable. • Per

as o first and last name o function/title o contact details o Other personal information related to the employment relationship, if applicable. • Personal information identified in the reconnaissance team reports (see item 4), including details about the allegations made and evidence supporting those allegations [2] • The date and time of calls (when the tip was received via the telephone hotline) • Any other information identified in the investigation findings and in the follow-up process to the report, e.g., information about criminal conduct or data about unlawful or improper conduct, if reported 3. Legal Basis The collection of the whistleblower's personal data in the case of a non-anonymous whistleblower is based on consent to the processing by the transmission of the data (implied consent) (Art. 6 para. 1 p. 1 lit. a GDPR). The collection, processing and transfer of personal data

cessing by the transmission of the data (implied consent) (Art. 6 para. 1 p. 1 lit. a GDPR). The collection, processing and transfer of personal data of the persons named in the notification serves the legitimate interests of Forschungszentrum Jülich (Art. 6 para. 1 p. 1 lit. f GDPR). It is a legitimate interest of Forschungszentrum Jülich to detect, process, stop and sanction violations of the law and serious breaches of duty by employees throughout the center, effectively and with a high degree of confidentiality, and to avert associated damage and liability risks for Forschungszentrum Jülich (Sections 30, 130 OWiG). Directive (EU) 2019/1937 ("EU Whistleblower Directive") and the future Whistleblower Protection Act (currently in draft form) also require the establishment of a whistleblower system in order to provide employees and third parties with the opportunity to provide protected

m) also require the establishment of a whistleblower system in order to provide employees and third parties with the opportunity to provide protected information about legal violations within the company in a suitable manner. The disclosure of personal data to other recipients in the case of non-anonymous reporting may be necessary due to a legal obligation (Art. 6 para. 1 p. 1 letter c GDPR). 4. Recipients of personal data and Transfer of data to a third country or an international organisation All personal data collected via the web-based software is only made accessible to those persons who have a legitimate need to process this data due to their function. The compliance officer of Forschungszentrum Jülich is in charge of the initial processing of incoming tips. If the tip is received via the telephone hotline, the tip will be recorded in the whistleblower system while preserving the

cessing of incoming tips. If the tip is received via the telephone hotline, the tip will be recorded in the whistleblower system while preserving the anonymity of the whistleblower. Employees who receive information are bound to secrecy (see below). At Forschungszentrum Jülich, only authorized employees from the following departments have access to the data (Clarification Team): • Compliance Officer of Forschungszentrum Jülich • Representation of the Compliance Officer • Required internal departments to be contacted in the course of the reconnaissance process In some cases, the company is required to disclose the data to authorities (such as those with legal or regulatory jurisdiction over the employer, law enforcement agencies and legal bodies) or external advisors (such as auditors, accountants, lawyers). If whistleblowers provide names or other personal data (non-anonymous information

dies) or external advisors (such as auditors, accountants, lawyers). If whistleblowers provide names or other personal data (non-anonymous information), their identity will not be disclosed - as far as legally possible - and it will also be ensured that no conclusions can be drawn about the identity of the whistleblower. If personal data is processed by external service providers, this is generally done on the basis of order processing contracts in accordance with Art. 28 GDPR. In these cases, we ensure that the processing of personal data is carried out in accordance with the provisions of the GDPR and that all persons authorized to process personal data have committed themselves to confidentiality or are subject to an appropriate legal duty of confidentiality. The whistleblower system is operated on our behalf by LegalTegrity GmbH, Platz der Einheit 2, 60327 Frankfurt/Main. There is no

ty of confidentiality. The whistleblower system is operated on our behalf by LegalTegrity GmbH, Platz der Einheit 2, 60327 Frankfurt/Main. There is no transfer of personal data to third countries (EU/EEA foreign countries). 5. Storage period, deletion of data The personal data will be retained in the respective proceedings for as long as is necessary for the clarification and final assessment, for a legitimate interest of Forschungszentrum Jülich or for a legal requirement. Afterwards, this data will be deleted in accordance with the legal requirements. The duration of storage depends in particular on the severity of the suspicion and the reported possible breach of duty. 6. Technical details when using the whistleblower system Communication between your computer and the whistleblower system takes place via an encrypted connection (SSL). The IP address of your computer is not stored duri

n between your computer and the whistleblower system takes place via an encrypted connection (SSL). The IP address of your computer is not stored during the use of the whistleblowing system. To maintain the connection between your computer and the whistleblower system, a cookie is stored on your computer, which [3] only contains the session ID. The cookie is only valid until the end of your session and becomes invalid when you close the browser. 7. Data subject rights You have the following rights in connection with the processing of personal data concerning you: • According to Art. 7 GDPR, you have the right to withdraw your consent to data processing at any time. The revocation of consent does not affect the lawfulness of the processing carried out on the basis of the consent until the revocation. • Pursuant to Art. 14 GDPR, if your data is collected without your knowledge (for example

arried out on the basis of the consent until the revocation. • Pursuant to Art. 14 GDPR, if your data is collected without your knowledge (for example, because you are involved in the whistleblowing procedure as an accused person), you have the right to be informed about the storage, the nature of the data, the purpose of the processing and the identity of the controller and, if applicable, the whistleblower (unless the whistleblowing was done anonymously). However, if there would be a significant risk that such information would jeopardize Forschungszentrum Jülich's ability to effectively investigate the allegation or gather the necessary evidence, this information may be postponed pursuant to Article 14 (5) sentence 1 lit. b GDPR in connection with Para 29 (1) BDSG for as long as this risk exists. The information must then be provided as soon as the reason for the postponement has ceas

ction with Para 29 (1) BDSG for as long as this risk exists. The information must then be provided as soon as the reason for the postponement has ceased to exist. • Pursuant to Art. 15 GDPR, you have the right to request information about the personal data concerning you that is processed by Forschungszentrum Jülich. Information that is subject to special secrecy within the meaning of Section 29 (1) BDSG is excluded from this. • Pursuant to Art. 16 GDPR, you have the right to request the immediate corr ection or completion of incorrect or incomplete data stored by us. • Pursuant to Art. 17 GDPR, you have the right to request the erasure of personal data concerning you that is stored by us, unless the processing is necessary for the exercise of the right to freedom of expression and information, for compliance with a legal obligation to which Forschungszentrum Jülich is subject, for the p

of the right to freedom of expression and information, for compliance with a legal obligation to which Forschungszentrum Jülich is subject, for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest, or for the establishment, exercise or defense of legal claims. • Pursuant to Art. 18 GDPR, you may request the restriction of the processing of your personal data if you contest the accuracy of such data or if the processing of such data is unlawful. • Pursuant to Art. 20 GDPR, you have the right to receive the personal data concerning you in a structured, common and machine-readable format, and to transmit this data to another controller without hindrance or to have it transmitted by us. • In accordance with Article 21 of the GDPR, you have the right to object to the processing of your personal data, insofar as there are grounds for doing so that arise from your particular situation.

have the right to object to the processing of your personal data, insofar as there are grounds for doing so that arise from your particular situation. Your data will then no longer be processed unless Forschungszentrum Jülich can demonstrate compelling grounds for the processing which override the interests, rights and freedoms of the data subject, or for the assertion, exercise or defense of legal claims. • Pursuant to Art. 77 GDPR in conjunction with. § Section 17 BDSG, you have the right to lodge a complaint against Forschungszentrum Jülich with the competent supervisory authority. This is: The Federal Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information. Graurheindorfer Str. 153, 53117 Bonn, Germany E-mail: poststelle@bfdi.bund.de 8. Automated decision making / profiling Your data will not be used for automated decision making or profiling.

Circular No. 6/2019 Handling Research Data [∗] Forschungszentrum Jülich conducts research on behalf society and therefore assumes responsibility for ensuring that the research findings can be used by society. We are committed to the principles of openly handling knowledge, research results, and technologies. Research data are an essential constituent of scientific output. They are necessary for the verifiability and reproducibility of research processes and results. Published research data have a long-term value for science and particularly the potential for extensive reuse. Simultaneously, they increase the visibility of our scientists and their work. The present guidelines are intended to achieve a common understanding at Forschungszentrum Jülich of how research data should be handled and ultimately to implement shared research data management. Well-documented research data that are ea

m Jülich of how research data should be handled and ultimately to implement shared research data management. Well-documented research data that are easy to find are fundamental to any research project. They help maintain the smooth operation and high quality of science as an enterprise, even in the face of complex collaborations and changes in personnel. For this reason, the management, storage, and sustainable provision of our research data must satisfy accepted standards, meet high requirements, and follow the rules of good scientific practice. Legal and ethical obligations must be observed and the particularities of scientific disciplines considered. Research data stored locally must be compatible with the institutional research data repository of Forschungszentrum Jülich as well as relevant national and international research data infrastructures. In these guidelines, software refers to all forms of program code (e.g. source code and associated documentation) and executable programs generated from these, which are developed, provided, and distributed within the scope of activities at Forschungszentrum Jülich. The development of software is part of a creative process and thus generates executable knowledge. It is an integral part of modern publication contexts consisting of written publication, data sets, and software. Furthermore, software development is an intellectual achievement protected by copyright and, in the context of research, an independent product of scientific work. Most scientists who write code are not trained software developers and often orient themselves to their immediate working environment and the experiences

t scientists who write code are not trained software developers and often orient themselves to their immediate working environment and the experiences of their colleagues when it comes to software engineering, without nece ssarily knowing the institution’s advisory services, tools, and best - practice experiences. In order to support these scientists in their independence and ability to act, these guidelines provide a framework for the development, management, and distribution of software at Forschungszentrum Jülich. The guidelines establish basic requirements for the development, management, and distribution of software, and describe practical procedures as well as corresponding examples and advisory services. The guidelines deal with the software life cycle [1], from software development and documentation to software distribution and maintenance. The guidelines aim to support the estab

software life cycle [1], from software development and documentation to software distribution and maintenance. The guidelines aim to support the establishment of modern software engineering methods at Forschungszentrum Jülich, which enable high standards in software development, software quality, and management. This professionalization will help to achieve more sustainability and promote good scientific practice in terms of verifiability and reproducibility of research results. These guidelines are primarily aimed at all developers and senior employees who are directly or indirectly involved in the development, management, and distribution of software at Forschungszentrum Jülich. The objective of the guidelines is to establish a tangible and sustainable use of programmed software at Forschungszentrum Jülich, to improve software quality, and to increase the appreciation of high-quality s

nd sustainable use of programmed software at Forschungszentrum Jülich, to improve software quality, and to increase the appreciation of high-quality software. The guidelines take into account the Helmholtz Association’s perspective on open science and are based on the “Model Policy on Sustainable Software at the Helmholtz Centers” [ 2] of the Helmholtz Association’s Open Science Working Group. They reflect and implement the Regulations for Upholding Good Scientific Practice at Forschungszentrum Jülich GmbH [3], the internal provisions of the directive “Publications by Forschungszentrum Jülich GmbH” [ 4 ], the “Guidelines for Handling Research Data at Forschungszentrum Jülich” [ 5], and the directive on information security (in preparation). In the spirit of open science, access to and reuse of software are the basis for traceability, verifiability, and reproducibility of scientific results. rum Jülich encourages researchers to make software openly accessible as “ open source ” software. At the start of a software project, however, the institute developing the software must consider the transfer path that will have the most effective impact on society and the economy. If there are commercial applications for the software, economic exploitation using proprietary licences may make more sense than an open source publication. Proprietary licences can also drive innovation (e.g. in a company) and secure a technological edge (e.g. for a spin-off). Open science, on the one hand, and commercial exploitation, on the other hand, are not contradictory, but complementary components of Forschungszentrum Jü lich’s innovation strategy. 3 Principles for the development, documentation, and distribution of software at Forschungszentrum Jülich Software and software projects differ from each ot

ciples for the development, documentation, and distribution of software at Forschungszentrum Jülich Software and software projects differ from each other both in terms of complexity and maturity. The complexity of a software project can result from technical aspects but also from management aspects, such as joint software development in a large consortium. In addition, the scientific field, the programming technologies used, the target group of the project, and the potential risk (e.g. liability in case of malfunction/failure) that may be associated with the final software product must be taken into account. In practice, software development is an iterative and ongoing activity that changes throughout the software life cycle . and quality standards regarding software quality, which are established by Forschungszentrum Jülich, are based on the application class of the software developed. At the beginning of a project, those responsible assign each software developed to an application class (AC) for which the measures described regarding quality and documentation must be adhered to. As experience has shown that many projects start small but then develop further, it is recommended to use standards of higher application classes from the very beginning. These application classes are based on the application classes proposed and implemented by the German Aerospace Center (DLR) [9]. 3.1 Classification according to application classes The application classes depend primarily on the intended use of the software and on whether and to what extent it will be distributed. Planned or required distribution of the software is a decisive criterion for the selection of the application class . However, the measures and standards for the corresponding application class mainly relate to software development and documentation. The complexity of a software project and the maturity of the software are therefore not necessarily reflected in the application classes . It is possible to switch from one application class to another but usually from lower to higher classes. It can therefore be helpful to orient yourself to the standards of the higher application class right from the beginning, if software distribution is not excluded. e criterion of the definition is that a software licence allows the comprehensive “free” use of the program and does not provide for any restriction of the licensees or areas of use, which means that it may be used by anyone for any purpose.
